plainMeaning: I now look at the five ways my final, willing fast-unto-death could be spoiled by lingering desire, and then I gather up every fault — big or small, known or unknown — in all twelve vows and let it dissolve.
recitation: apacchima maraṇāṁtika saṁleṣaṇā-taṇā pāṁca atiyāra śodhuṁ. ihalogā saṁsappaoge, paralogā saṁsappaoge, jiviyā saṁsappaoge, maraṇā saṁsappaoge, kāmabhogā saṁsappaoge, ihaloka āśrī ṛddhi, vṛddhi-taṇī āśaṁsā vāṁchā kīdhī hoya; paraloka āśrī iṁdra, cakravartī-taṇī āśaṁsā vāṁchā kīdhī hoya. jivitavya ane maraṇā-taṇī āśaṁsā vāṁchā kīdhī hoya. kāma bhoga-taṇī āśaṁsā vāṁchā kīdhī hoya. aneruṁ e saṁleṣaṇā viṣe. ॥ evaṁkāre śrī samyaktva-mūla bāra vrata viṣe paṁcyāśī atiyāra-māṁhe jiko koi atiyāra, anācāra, atikrama, vyatikrama huo hoya, tathā jāṇatāṁ ajāṇatāṁ sūkṣma, bādara, kāno, mātrā, miṁḍī, pada, akṣara, ocho, adhiko, haḷavo, bhārī, āgala, pāchala, kahyo kahevāṇo hoya. te savi huṁ mane, vacane, kāyāe karī micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ.

wordByWord.gloss: final, last (the very last fast of a lifetime)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit apacchima < Sanskrit apaścima ('last, hindmost'); the technical name of the death-bed sallekhanā.

wordByWord.gloss: occurring at the point of death; terminal
of the sallekhanā (peaceful fast unto death)
wordByWord.etymology: sallekhanā + Old-Gujarati genitive postposition -taṇā ('of, belonging to').
wordByWord.notes: saṁleṣaṇā is glossed in the preface; only the genitive ending is parsed here.

wordByWord.etymology: Book's spelling of atichāra (with ya), per the orthography rule.
wordByWord.notes: Glossary term; not re-defined.

wordByWord.gloss: I purify / I examine and cleanse
wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati śodhvuṁ ('to cleanse, scrutinize') < Sanskrit √śudh; 1st-person present 'I purify'.
wordByWord.notes: The recurring formulaic verb that opens each vow's atichāra-list.

wordByWord.gloss: applying longing toward this world (first sallekhanā transgression)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit ihaloga (Skt iha-loka, 'this world') + āsaṁsā-paoga (Skt āśaṁsā-prayoga, 'engaging/applying of expectation'); locative -e.
wordByWord.notes: saṁsappaoge = āśaṁsā-prayoga; the doubling is the book's Prakrit reading, preserved verbatim.

wordByWord.gloss: applying longing toward the next world (second transgression)
applying longing to go on living (third transgression)
applying longing for death to come quickly (fourth transgression)

wordByWord.notes: These five are the Prakrit āgamic names; the lines that follow gloss each in Gujarati.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati āśrī < Sanskrit āśritya ('having resorted to, concerning').

wordByWord.notes: The Gujarati gloss-word for the Prakrit -saṁsa above.
Paired with āśaṁsā as a near-synonym doublet ('longing-and-craving').

wordByWord.etymology: Old-Gujarati past participle of karavuṁ ('to do'), fem. sg. agreeing with vāṁchā.

wordByWord.notes: 'kīdhī hoya' = 'should it have been done' — the confessional conditional refrain.

wordByWord.gloss: the next world, the life beyond
Indra, king of the gods (an exalted heavenly rebirth)
wordByWord.notes: Named as the supreme other-worldly prize one must not crave.
Indra and cakravartin together symbolize the highest heavenly and earthly glory.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit jīvitavya ('that which is to be lived, life').
wordByWord.notes: Gujarati gloss of jiviya-saṁsa above.
The sweep-up word that gathers unnamed faults.
Closing 'aneruṁ e saṁleṣaṇā viṣe' = 'anything else regarding the sallekhanā'.
Part of the text per the orthography rule.
Opens the grand summing-up of all twelve vows.
Recalls the opening doctrine: the twelve vows grow from samyaktva.
(recurs)
The book's authoritative total of atichāras across the twelve vows; see notes.
First of a four-fold gradation of fault.
The severest grade: outright violation.
First stage of the classic atikrama–vyatikrama–atichāra–anācāra ladder of breach.
Second stage of the same ladder.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati present participle of jāṇavuṁ ('to know').

wordByWord.notes: One axis of the gross/subtle pair noted in the preface.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati script-term for the ā-mātrā vertical mark.
wordByWord.notes: Begins the list of recitation errors confessed (mis-pronounced diacritics).

wordByWord.gloss: a vowel-sign / measure of a syllable

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati miṁḍī, the small superscript dot.

wordByWord.gloss: spoken (by me) or caused to be spoken
wordByWord.etymology: kahyo (active past) + kahevāṇo (passive/causative past) of kahevuṁ ('to say').
wordByWord.notes: Covers faults of one's own recitation and of having others misrecite.
(recurs)
First of the mana–vacana–kāyā triad.
'mane vacane kāyāe karī' = 'committed by mind, speech and body'.

wordByWord.gloss: may my misdeed come to nothing
wordByWord.etymology: Glossary term; the sealing Prakrit formula of repentance.
wordByWord.notes: Closes the entire bṛhad-atiyāra confession.

literalTranslation: I purify the five transgressions of the final, death-time sallekhanā: applying longing toward this world, toward the next world, toward continued life, toward death, toward sense-pleasures. If, with reference to this world, a longing or craving for affluence and increase has been made; if, with reference to the next world, a longing or craving for (rebirth as) Indra or a cakravartin has been made; if a longing or craving for living and for death has been made; if a longing or craving for sensual enjoyment has been made — and anything else regarding the sallekhanā. Thus, within the twelve vows rooted in right faith, among their eighty-five transgressions, whatever transgression, misconduct, first or further overstepping may have occurred; and whether knowingly or unknowingly, subtle or gross, a kāno, a vowel-mark, an anusvāra-dot, a word or a syllable said too little or too much, too lightly or too heavily, before or after, spoken or caused to be spoken — all of that, I, by mind, speech and body, may my misdeed come to nothing.
idiomaticTranslation: Now I cleanse the five faults that can spoil the last, willing fast of my life: clinging to this world, hankering after the next, craving to keep living, wishing death would hurry, or yearning for pleasures of the senses. Should I have hoped, looking to this life, for wealth and worldly increase; should I have hoped, looking to the life beyond, to be reborn an Indra or a world-emperor; should I have craved either life or death, or any sensual joy — and whatever else touches this final fast — I confess it. So too, within the twelve vows that grow from right faith, among their full eighty-five transgressions: every breach great or small, every overstepping, every fault done knowingly or unknowingly, subtle or gross — every misread stroke, vowel, dot, word or syllable, said short or long, soft or loud, ahead or behind, by my own tongue or another's — for all of it, in mind, speech and body, may my misdeed come to nothing.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This climactic section does two things at once. First it completes the vow-by-vow march begun in earlier verses by reaching sallekhanā — not itself one of the twelve vows but the practitioner's final examination, the death-bed fast whose only enemy is desire. Its five atichāras are not acts of violence or theft but five subtle leanings of the heart: toward this world (ihaloga), the next (paraloga), more life (jīviya), quicker death (maraṇa), or sensual pleasure (kāmabhoga). The Prakrit āgamic names are immediately re-glossed in homely Gujarati: hoping for ṛddhi and vṛddhi here, for rebirth as Indra or a cakravartin hereafter, or for life, death, or enjoyment. The lesson is exact — even the holiest act is corrupted the instant it is performed for a reward. Second, the verse turns from the particular to the total. 'Evaṁkāre' gathers the whole confession: within the twelve samyaktva-rooted vows and their eighty-five transgressions, the reciter sweeps up not only every grade of fault (atiyāra, anācāra, atikrama, vyatikrama) but, strikingly, every error of recitation itself — a misplaced kāno, mātrā, anusvāra-dot, a syllable short or long, soft or loud, out of order, spoken or made to be spoken. Confession here audits even the act of confessing. All of it is then dissolved in the mana–vacana–kāyā triad and sealed with micchā mi dukkaḍaṁ, returning the long bṛhad-atiyāra to the same refrain that closed every vow.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The five atichāras here follow the Śvetāmbara āgamic enumeration (Uvāsagadasāo / Āvaśyaka tradition) — ihaloka-, paraloka-, jīvita-, maraṇa-, kāmabhoga-āśaṁsā-prayoga — rather than the Tattvārtha-sūtra 7.32 set (jīvitāśaṁsā, maraṇāśaṁsā, mitrānurāga, sukhānubandha, nidāna); both guard the same principle, that sallekhanā must be free of nidāna (desire-for-fruit). Doctrinally this verse fixes sallekhanā as the consummation of aparigraha and samyaktva: longing, not death, is the true defect. The closing tally then absorbs the five ācāras (jñāna, darśana, [cāritra], tapa, vīrya) — note the named recitation-faults are precisely jñānācāra lapses — affirming that right conduct extends even to how scripture is voiced.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: A reciter today need not be dying to use this verse: it asks whether any good deed — fasting, worship, even this very pratikramaṇa — is being done for prosperity, status, comfort, or relief, and invites letting that motive go. Its second half gives explicit permission to confess clumsy recitation — wrong stress, dropped syllables, words out of order — so that fear of imperfect Prakrit never becomes a barrier to sincere atonement.

crossReferences.text: Pañcācāra — jñāna, darśana, cāritra, tapa, vīrya ācāra and their atichāras

sources.title: Sallekhana
sources.consultedFor: The five atichāras of sallekhanā and the doctrine that the fast must be free of desire/nidāna.

sources.title: Tattvartha Sutra (vows and their transgressions, ch. 7)
sources.consultedFor: The classical 7.32 enumeration of sallekhanā transgressions and the framework of vow-atichāras.

sources.title: Āśaṁsā / āśaṁsā-prayoga (definition)
sources.consultedFor: Glossing the Prakrit -saṁsappaoga as Sanskrit āśaṁsā-prayoga ('application of longing/expectation').

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: The verbatim Gujarati text, the eighty-five (pañcyāśī) atichāra total, and the closing five-ācāra confession.

notes: The verse's five Prakrit terms follow the Śvetāmbara āgamic set (ihaloga/paraloga/jīviya/maraṇa/kāmabhoga āsaṁsā-paoga), which differs from the Tattvārtha-sūtra 7.32 set; both are noted in doctrinalContext. 'saṁsappaoge' is read as āśaṁsā-prayoga (locative); the conjunct doubling is the book's own and is preserved. The total 'pañcyāśī' (85) is honored as authoritative for this edition though other reference works cite 84/124 under different counting conventions (per the preface notes). The four-grade fault ladder atikrama–vyatikrama–atichāra–anācāra is interpreted per classical usage; the text lists them without internal definition. The numbering jump 12→14 is editorial (the sallekhanā + five-ācāra material carries number 14) and implies no missing verse 13. The script-error terms (kāno, mātrā, miṁḍī) are Gujarati orthographic names rendered descriptively in English.
